task: I’m looking at a small property where the seller’s info sounds fine, but I don’t totally trust it and I don’t want to walk into a surprise after closing. What’s the smartest way to decide what needs to be checked first so I’m not buying blind?

turns.agent_response:
Use a **three-pass filter**: **money, condition, and paper**.

## 1) Money first
Check whether the income story is real:
- rent roll
- actual collections
- delinquency
- leases and amendments
- estoppels if possible
- trailing 12
- bank statements
- operating expenses from bills

**Goal:** confirm the property actually produces the cash the seller claims.

## 2) Condition second
Check whether the building is hiding cost:
- roof
- structure
- plumbing
- electrical
- HVAC
- drainage
- envelope
- parking / site
- visible code issues
- recent repairs

**Goal:** find deferred maintenance or hidden CapEx before you own it.

## 3) Paper third
Check whether the deal can close cleanly:
- title prelim
- liens
- easements
- zoning / use
- permits
- code notices
- insurance claims
- seller disclosures
- service contracts

**Goal:** make sure there isn’t a legal or administrative problem that blows up closing or refinance.

## What to check first if you don’t trust the seller
Start with the thing most likely to change the price:
1. **Income** if it’s an income property
2. **Condition** if the building is older or visibly tired
3. **Paper** if there’s any hint of title, permit, or code trouble

## Fast rule
If the seller’s story sounds fine but you don’t trust it:
- verify cash
- verify condition
- verify title / permits

If any one of those fails, slow down.

If you want, I can turn this into a **simple due diligence order of operations** for a small property.

user_simulator.text:
Direction’s right. But I’d want the order tighter than that, because “check everything” is how people waste time.

My sequence:
1. **Paper first if anything smells off** — title prelim, liens, permits, code, zoning, easements. If there’s a hard stop there, no point burning money on deep property work yet.
2. **Cash flow second** — rent roll, actual collections, T-12, bank deposits, delinquency, leases. Seller numbers are just a story until they match deposits.
3. **Condition third** — roof, structure, plumbing, electrical, HVAC, drainage, site, any obvious deferred maintenance.

If it’s a small property and the seller’s info already feels slippery, I’d do a cheap screen first: title + permit/code + rent verification. That usually tells you fast whether you’re dealing with a clean deal or a mess dressed up as one.

What I would not do is spend on a full inspection before I know the paper and income are basically real. That’s just donating money to find out later the deal never should’ve been touched.

turns.agent_response:
Use this order:

## 1) Paper first if anything smells off
Check:
- title prelim
- liens
- permits
- code
- zoning
- easements

If there’s a hard stop here, stop before deeper work.

## 2) Cash flow second
Check:
- rent roll
- actual collections
- T-12
- bank deposits
- delinquency
- leases

Seller numbers only matter if they match deposits.

## 3) Condition third
Check:
- roof
- structure
- plumbing
- electrical
- HVAC
- drainage
- site
- obvious deferred maintenance

## Cheap screen first
If the seller feels slippery, do the cheap screen first:
- title
- permit/code
- rent verification

That usually tells you fast whether it’s clean or a mess.

## Fast rule
Don’t spend on a full inspection before:
- paper is basically clean
- income is basically real

If you want, I can turn this into a **one-page due diligence sequence** you can use on a small property.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the right sequence.

Paper first when there’s any smell on it. Cash second. Condition last.

And the cheap screen is the part people skip when they’re overeager: title prelim, permit/code, rent verification. That’s enough to tell you if the thing is worth spending real money on.

I’d keep the rule even tighter:
- hard stop on paper = walk or renegotiate
- income doesn’t match deposits = assume the story is fiction
- condition work only after the first two clear enough to justify it

That’s the clean way to avoid buying blind.
